messages.content: hi! we have a bug where if a *lot* of thoughts are streamed (like 50k) the main slowdown factor is the rerendering of the thought bubble.
Let me find the thought bubble rendering code to understand the current implementation.

{"success": true, "message": "Found 10 files matching 'reasoning|thought|thinking'", "matches": [{"filepath": "forge/prompts/manager.py", "match_count": 11, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/prompts/system.py", "match_count": 8, "first_match": "thinking"}, {"filepath": "forge/runtime/events.py", "match_count": 6, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/runtime/llm_backend.py", "match_count": 10, "first_match": "thinking"}, {"filepath": "forge/session/live_session.py", "match_count": 22, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/session/manager.py", "match_count": 1, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/tools/builtin/think.py", "match_count": 6, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/ui/ai_chat_widget.py", "match_count": 23, "first_match": "thought"}, {"filepath": "forge/ui/chat_streaming.py", "match_count": 21, "first_match": "reasoning"}, {"filepath": "forge/ui/chat_styles.py", "match_count": 12, "first_match": "reasoning"}], "action": "update_context", "add": ["forge/prompts/manager.py", "forge/prompts/system.py", "forge/runtime/events.py", "forge/runtime/llm_backend.py", "forge/session/live_session.py", "forge/session/manager.py", "forge/tools/builtin/think.py", "forge/ui/ai_chat_widget.py", "forge/ui/chat_streaming.py", "forge/ui/chat_styles.py"], "remove": []}

Let me analyze the bug. Looking at `build_reasoning_chunk_js` in `chat_streaming.py`:

The problem is clear. Every reasoning chunk triggers `build_reasoning_chunk_js(self.runner.streaming_reasoning)` — which sends the **entire accumulated reasoning text** to JavaScript via `contentSpan.innerText = \`{escaped}\``. So with 50k of thoughts streamed in many small chunks, each chunk re-sends and re-renders the *full* accumulated text. That's O(n²) in the total reasoning size — the classic append-by-rewriting-everything problem.

The fix: only send the **new chunk** and append it to the DOM, rather than resending the whole accumulated string each time.

Let me look at the flow:
- `_on_runner_reasoning_chunk` (in `ai_chat_widget.py`) is called with the individual `chunk`, but then calls `build_reasoning_chunk_js(self.runner.streaming_reasoning)` — using the accumulated string.

I'll change it to pass just the delta chunk and append via `appendData`/text node, while keeping a fallback to set full content when the bubble is first created (or empty). Let me restate the key design:

1. `build_reasoning_chunk_js` gets a new signature accepting the delta chunk (and whether it's the first chunk to establish the bubble).
2. On append, use `textContent +=` on the content span (or better, append a text node) so we don't reserialize.

Actually even `contentSpan.textContent += chunk` reads the existing content back. The cheapest is `appendChild(document.createTextNode(chunk))`. Let me use that.

Let me update the caller.

{"1_edits": [{"1_filepath": "forge/ui/ai_chat_widget.py", "2_search": "    def _on_runner_reasoning_chunk(self, chunk: str) -> None:
        """Handle reasoning/thinking chunk from runner.

        Renders the accumulated reasoning into a thought bubble at the top
        of the streaming message. The bubble auto-collapses when the actual
        response begins (see `_on_runner_chunk`).
        """
        js_code = build_reasoning_chunk_js(self.runner.streaming_reasoning)
        self.chat_view.page().runJavaScript(js_code)", "3_replace": "    def _on_runner_reasoning_chunk(self, chunk: str) -> None:
        """Handle reasoning/thinking chunk from runner.

        Appends the new reasoning delta to a thought bubble at the top of the
        streaming message. The bubble auto-collapses when the actual response
        begins (see `_on_runner_chunk`).

        We pass only the delta `chunk` (not the accumulated
        `streaming_reasoning`) so the bubble is built up incrementally in the
        DOM. Resending the full accumulated text on every chunk is O(n²) and
        was the dominant slowdown when a model streams tens of thousands of
        tokens of reasoning.
        """
        js_code = build_reasoning_chunk_js(chunk)
        self.chat_view.page().runJavaScript(js_code)"}]}

[id 6] The only caller is the one I already updated. Both the buffered-event path (`_handle_runner_event` → `ReasoningChunkEvent` → `_on_runner_reasoning_chunk(event.chunk)`) and the live-signal path (`reasoning_chunk_received` → `_on_runner_reasoning_chunk`) pass the individual `chunk`, so my delta-append change is correct for both.

There's one edge case worth noting: on **attach mid-stream**, the snapshot returns the accumulated `streaming_reasoning`, but nothing re-renders it into the bubble (that was already true before my change — the old code also only rendered on new chunks). So attaching mid-thought would miss already-streamed reasoning. That's a pre-existing behavior, not a regression from this fix, and it's the rare case (attaching a detached session mid-reasoning). I'll leave it as-is to keep the fix focused.

Let me run the streaming-related tests to confirm nothing broke.

function.arguments:
{"1_message": "Fix O(n²) thought-bubble rerender by appending reasoning deltas

The reasoning thought bubble was rebuilt on every streamed chunk by
resending the entire accumulated reasoning string to JS and setting
contentSpan.innerText. With tens of thousands of tokens streamed in many
small chunks, that made rendering O(n²) and was the dominant slowdown.

build_reasoning_chunk_js now takes just the new delta chunk and appends it
as a text node (O(n) total). The caller passes the per-chunk delta instead
of runner.streaming_reasoning."}

messages.content: {"success": true, "message": "Committed: 2 file(s) modified/created", "commit": "eb6e610f4abd", "side_effects": ["mid_turn_commit"]}

Done! Fixed the O(n²) thought-bubble slowdown.

**The bug:** every streamed reasoning chunk called `build_reasoning_chunk_js(self.runner.streaming_reasoning)` with the *entire accumulated* reasoning text, then did `contentSpan.innerText = <full text>` in JS. So with 50k of thoughts arriving in many small chunks, each chunk re-serialized and re-rendered the whole growing string — quadratic total work, dominating the render time.

**The fix (2 files):**
- `chat_streaming.py` — `build_reasoning_chunk_js` now takes just the new **delta** chunk and appends it as a text node (`contentSpan.appendChild(document.createTextNode(...))`) instead of rewriting the full content. Linear total work.
- `ai_chat_widget.py` — `_on_runner_reasoning_chunk` now passes the per-chunk `chunk` instead of `self.runner.streaming_reasoning`.

Both event paths (live signal and buffered `ReasoningChunkEvent`) already carry the individual delta, so the change is correct for both.

**One thing I left alone (flagging it):** on *attach mid-stream*, the snapshot returns the accumulated `streaming_reasoning`, but nothing re-paints it into the bubble — so attaching a detached session mid-reasoning would miss already-streamed thought text. That's pre-existing behavior (the old code also only painted on new chunks), not a regression, so I kept the fix focused. Let me know if you'd like me to also handle that attach case.

All 35 streaming/chat/reasoning tests pass; committed as `eb6e610f`.
✅ Generated summaries for 90 files

function.arguments:
{"1_scratchpad": "Where does memory grow as thoughts stream?

1. Python side (live_session.py):
   - `append_streaming_reasoning` does `self.streaming_reasoning += chunk`. This accumulates the full reasoning string. For 50k tokens that's maybe ~200KB of text — not huge, but it's kept for the whole turn. Also each `+=` creates a new string (CPython optimizes in-place sometimes). Not the main memory driver.
   - Each chunk emits a `ReasoningChunkEvent(chunk)`. If DETACHED, these buffer in `self._event_buffer` deque forever until attach. But normally attached. When attached, `_emit_event` emits signal directly, no buffer growth.

2. The real suspect: the JS/WebEngine side. Every reasoning chunk calls `self.chat_view.page().runJavaScript(js_code)`. Each call ships a JS string to the web engine. With the delta fix, js_code is small now. But runJavaScript queues work on the web engine. If chunks arrive faster than the web engine processes them, they queue up. Still, that's transient.

3. The DOM: we now append a text node per chunk. 50k tokens in small chunks = potentially thousands of text nodes in the DOM. That's DOM memory growth but bounded and not "rapid" per se.

Actually — the biggest memory issue: `streaming_reasoning` accumulates but is NEVER cleared appropriately? Let's check: start_streaming sets streaming_reasoning="". finish_streaming doesn't clear it. cancel() clears streaming_content but NOT streaming_reasoning. So after a turn, streaming_reasoning stays holding the full reasoning text. That's a leak of one turn's worth, overwritten next turn. Minor.

But the user says "as we stream thoughts the memory use grows rapidly". Rapid growth during streaming. The most likely culprit given our recent change: we now `contentSpan.appendChild(document.createTextNode(...))` per chunk. That's fine. But the PYTHON `streaming_reasoning += chunk` still accumulates — and we don't even USE streaming_reasoning for rendering anymore (we render deltas)! The only remaining use of streaming_reasoning is the check in `_on_runner_chunk`: `if self.runner.streaming_reasoning and not ...collapsed`. That just needs a boolean "did we have any reasoning", not the full text.

And the snapshot/attach returns streaming_reasoning. 

So the accumulation of streaming_reasoning is now essentially only needed as a "was there any reasoning" flag plus attach snapshot. But keeping the full string is what grows memory. However 200KB isn't "rapid".

Let me reconsider. The MOST likely rapid memory growth: `runJavaScript` calls. QtWebEngine's runJavaScript with a callback / without — each call may retain. But more importantly, the actual issue is probably that we emit a Qt signal per chunk and each signal triggers a runJavaScript. If the model streams truly 50k tokens as thousands of tiny chunks, we make thousands of runJavaScript calls, each crossing the process boundary to the render process. That's CPU, and the render process DOM grows.

Actually, I think I'm overthinking. Let me look at what actually accumulates unboundedly. The clue "memory grows rapidly" during a single stream. Candidates that grow O(n) with chunks within one turn:
- streaming_reasoning string (grows with total text — linear, ~bytes of text)
- DOM text nodes (grows with chunk count)
- event buffer (only if detached)

None "rapid" unless... the request log? Every chunk... no.

Wait — is there something that stores every chunk? Let me check if streaming_reasoning is being appended to messages. append_streaming_reasoning does NOT update messages (unlike append_streaming_chunk which calls update_last_assistant_message). Good.

I think the cleanest, most defensible memory fix: don't accumulate the full `streaming_reasoning` string when it's only used as a boolean flag + rendering deltas. Replace it with a counter/flag, OR at minimum stop growing it. But attach() returns it in the snapshot and the ReasoningChunkEvent path... Actually for correctness of attach mid-stream (which I flagged earlier), keeping accumulated text is arguably useful. But it's not the memory hog.

Let me actually look for the real accumulation. I should load the files and check. Let me check the request_log and whether chunks get logged, and check runJavaScript usage. But I don't want to guess — per CLAUDE.md, when uncertain, investigate. Let me scout / grep the relevant areas: runJavaScript callback retention, and whether there's a per-chunk callback list.

Given the context, the strongest concrete lead: `runJavaScript(js_code)` called once per chunk with NO callback is fine, but the JS strings themselves are transient. The DOM growth (thousands of text nodes) IS real and unbounded-per-turn. We could coalesce chunks.

Actually the best memory improvement that's also robust: batch/coalesce reasoning chunks before shipping to JS. Instead of one runJavaScript per tiny chunk, buffer deltas and flush on a timer. This reduces both the number of runJavaScript calls AND the number of DOM text nodes (fewer, larger appends). That addresses "memory grows rapidly" if the growth is from runJavaScript queuing / DOM node count.

But I shouldn't guess blindly. Let me look at the actual code paths for how chunks flow and whether anything retains them. I'll grep for runJavaScript and streaming_reasoning usages, and load request_log.py.", "2_conclusion": "Need to investigate before fixing. Key facts I've established: `append_streaming_reasoning` does `streaming_reasoning += chunk` (accumulates full text all turn, never cleared by finish_streaming/cancel), and `streaming_reasoning` is now only actually needed as a "was there any reasoning?" flag (the render path uses deltas after my last fix) plus the attach() snapshot. Candidates for rapid memory growth during a single stream: (1) the accumulating `streaming_reasoning` string, (2) unbounded DOM text-node count (one appendChild per chunk), (3) one `runJavaScript` call per tiny chunk queuing in QtWebEngine. I must investigate the actual accumulation rather than guess — check request logging of chunks and runJavaScript usage."}

I have the facts I need. Let me identify the memory sources during thought streaming:

**Source 1 — `streaming_reasoning` accumulates unboundedly and is never cleared.** `append_streaming_reasoning` does `self.streaming_reasoning += chunk` for the whole turn. After my last fix, this string is only used as (a) a boolean "was there any reasoning?" flag in `_on_runner_chunk`, and (b) the `attach()` snapshot. Keeping 50k+ of text just to answer "was there any?" is wasteful — but on its own it's one copy of the text, not *rapid* growth. Still worth fixing.

**Source 2 — the real rapid growth: DOM text-node explosion + one `runJavaScript` per chunk.** Every reasoning chunk now does `contentSpan.appendChild(document.createTextNode(chunk))`. With tens of thousands of tiny chunks, that's tens of thousands of separate DOM text nodes, each with per-node overhead in the QtWebEngine render process — memory grows far faster than the raw text size. Plus each chunk is a separate `runJavaScript` marshalled across the IPC boundary to the render process.

The fix for both: **buffer/coalesce reasoning chunks on the Python side** and flush to the webview on a timer (e.g. every ~100ms), appending the coalesced batch as a *single* text node. This collapses thousands of tiny nodes/IPC calls into a few dozen. And since the render bubble is built incrementally, I can stop accumulating the full `streaming_reasoning` string — replace it with a simple `has_reasoning` flag.

Let me confirm the `_on_runner_chunk` collapse-trigger and the cancel path before editing.

hm hang on- can you coalesce on the js side instead? I want to keep the py side simple if possible.

Good call — JS-side coalescing keeps Python dead simple (still one `runJavaScript` per chunk, but the *expensive* part, DOM mutation + layout, gets batched via `requestAnimationFrame`). Let me restate the plan:

- Keep `_on_runner_reasoning_chunk` exactly as-is (passes the delta `chunk`).
- In `build_reasoning_chunk_js`, instead of appending a text node immediately, push the delta into a per-bubble pending buffer (`bubble._pendingReasoning`) and schedule a single `requestAnimationFrame` flush. The flush appends all buffered deltas as **one** text node and does the scroll. This collapses thousands of tiny DOM mutations/layouts per frame into one, which is what drives the render-process memory/CPU growth.

This leaves the Python `streaming_reasoning += chunk` accumulation alone (you asked to keep Py simple), and it's a pure-JS change.

function.arguments:
{"1_message": "Coalesce reasoning-bubble DOM updates via requestAnimationFrame

Appending a text node and scrolling on every streamed reasoning chunk forced
thousands of separate DOM mutations and layout/paint passes when a model
streams tens of thousands of reasoning tokens, ballooning the QtWebEngine
render process memory/CPU.

build_reasoning_chunk_js now buffers each delta on the bubble element
(bubble._pendingReasoning) and flushes at most once per animation frame,
appending a frame's worth of deltas as a single text node with a single
scroll. Python stays simple — still one runJavaScript per chunk; the
expensive DOM work is batched in JS."}

messages.content: {"success": true, "message": "Committed: 1 file(s) modified/created", "commit": "b3bf1769a4dd", "side_effects": ["mid_turn_commit"]}

Both fixes are done and committed. Here's the summary of this second fix:

**The memory-growth cause:** even after the O(n²) render fix, every streamed reasoning chunk still did an immediate `appendChild(document.createTextNode(...))` plus a scroll. With tens of thousands of tiny chunks, that's tens of thousands of separate DOM mutations and layout/paint passes — the QtWebEngine render process balloons in memory/CPU from all that per-chunk churn.

**The fix (JS-side only, per your request):** `build_reasoning_chunk_js` now buffers each delta on the bubble element (`bubble._pendingReasoning`) and schedules a single `requestAnimationFrame` flush. The flush appends a whole frame's worth of deltas as *one* text node with *one* scroll. Python stays dead simple — still one `runJavaScript` per chunk; only the expensive DOM work is batched.

All 35 streaming/chat/reasoning tests pass. Committed as `b3bf1769`.

One note worth flagging: this batches the *DOM* work but Python still marshals one `runJavaScript` call per chunk across the IPC boundary. If profiling later shows that IPC itself is a bottleneck (not just the DOM), the next lever would be Python-side coalescing — but per your steer we've kept Python untouched, and the DOM batching addresses the render-process memory growth you described. Let me know if you'd like me to profile further or stop here.
